title: Traveling with Kyle - Indonesia Part 2, Malasia and Singapore

transcript.text: Whoops, all right, Indonesia, south side of Neos, I stayed at one bungalow, and then I went to another bungalow for a little bit, because it was closer, a little bit closer to the waves, and I was in a room, and then I decided to go back to the original one that I stayed in, and the next day, the bungalow that I was staying in completely burned down to the ground, so I was kind of lucky of me, and these two little monkeys that were next to me, unfortunately, they were tied up and they burned down also.

The people who had the original bungalow bought a monkey, and they tied it in front of where I lived for a while, and so I played with him, and he'd be in my room, but he wasn't really, I don't know if I can recommend having a monkey, because they jump on top of you, and he was trying to poke my eyes out, and he was scratching me and biting me, and, yeah, he was cute, though.

And, oh, and for four days, we did a, we rented a boat, or chartered a boat, with a bunch of these, a few of these American kids, to do these other, other waves to surf on, but I had burnt, it was when I first got there, but I had burnt my nose when I did that canoe trip around Lake Toba.

I forgot to bring sunscreen, and I guess I was at the equator, and to the point where my whole nose was a scab, and even both of my legs completely boiled up in these big, like, bubbles of water, and so I pretty much couldn't surf.

I surfed maybe a little bit of one day, and I had to stay inside, because my nose was raw, but then we went to these other islands, and we swam around, and it was fun, and this phosphorescence, when you move your hand, it all lights up, and we were on this little beach, and there were lots of, all the rocks were moving around, because they were all crabs, crab shells.

It was pretty trippy, and then we went back to Neos, I was, I ended up being there for a month and a half surfing, and then I rode my bike back to the original town, I took the boats in, but boat too, rode the boat back, but I didn't ride my bike across Sumatra again, because I had already done that trip, so I just threw my bike on the roof, and took the bus back.

I probably should have, if I had to do it again, I probably should have just ridden my bike, continued south in Sumatra, and climbed to the top of one of this active volcano, but I decided to go back, because I wanted to, I wanted to see Kuala Lumpur, so I went back, and I rode my bike into Kuala Lumpur, and I saw the highest building of the world at the time, which is on top of this tower next to it, and, um, but Kuala Lumpur, I guess, is a big city, but it didn't look very big to me.

It's one of those cities that's populated, but isn't large, and, um, and then I tried to ride my bike from Kuala Lumpur down to Singapore, but it was only highway, which was illegal for bikes, and I couldn't find any frontage roads, so I had to take the bus all the way to Singapore, well, to the border of Singapore, because Singapore officially is its own country, so then I rode my bike, like, at midnight, all the way across Singapore, and I was riding it for four hours, trying to look for the central Singapore,

City, but I kept getting, like, veered off from the wrong exit or something, and then I finally kind of decided to just camp out in some, you know, grassy patch, and I saw a mattress, so I laid on top of the mattress and slept there, and got up, and I ended up realizing I was right next to the main city, so I went down there, and the Indian section got around for a couple days.

Singapore was, Singapore was a very interesting place, because it's very clean, everything was, the roads, everything's immaculate, so I felt like I was in the first world country, and, um, but it was still pretty well priced, it was like $8 a night for the hotel I was in, and I took a bus ride all over the whole city, just to look at it, for some reason I didn't feel like riding my bike, I don't know, I'm not like that now, I'd ride my bike any day now.

Um, for example, it's weird, when I was in Korea, when I was living in Seoul for five and a half months, I didn't ride my bike around at all, only one of my brother was there, it was weird, I took the bus, I can't explain why, definitely wouldn't do that now.
So anyways, then I did a tour of the southern part, there's this, uh, I guess, where all the old military installation was, and some interesting, I can't remember what it was, but I did a tour there.

So I was there for a couple, for maybe four days, and then I took the boat down to Batam, a small island in Indonesia, and, uh, Indonesia, on this island, it was, Indonesia is a third world country, like, as poor as anything, the poorest country in Asia, it's kind of like Africa, really poor.

Yet, on Batam, you look north, and you can see the skyscrapers of Singapore, which must have been really bizarre for the Indonesians, because they could never go there, they didn't know anything of the first world, yet they can actually see the huge towers of the first world.

So I stopped there, and there was a girl there who, uh, who's, like, invited me to go up to her house, so I went up with her to her house, and, or she told me where her house was, because I stayed at a hotel the first night, and then, so I was, I walked up to the top of some hill, and I saw these ladies living in a hut, the most basic hut you can imagine, made out of toothpicks, basically, and then, you know, their outhouse was like,

A few feet away, and they were really, they wanted me to bring, they brought me to their house, and they wanted me to stay with them, and, but they looked really, uh, kind of ratty, and, so I was like, I don't know, thanks, and so I went over to this house, this girl's house, and I was hanging out with her family, the whole family was there, and, uh, just, I guess, asking me questions, basically, the whole time, interviewing me, and, uh, and then they said, where are you going to stay, and I said, well, here,

Because I thought that's what she said, and they were like, oh, no, you can't stay here, and I said, oh, okay, well, I guess I'll go back to my hotel, and they said, oh, wait, actually, you can stay in the office, so I stayed in the office, and, um, and then I went down, like, I walked, I rode my bike around the town, that's kind of what I like to do, just to check out the landscape, for me, traveling is seeing landscape, and so that's why it's great to travel with a bike, and then I took a, um,
A boat, which was a whole day's ride, down to, uh, Java, to Sumat, to Jakarta, um, and I got myself a bed, and the boats they have, they're huge boats,
Boats, like, Titanic-sized boats, maybe not that big, but, um, and it was only, like, three dollars for a whole day's journey, which, that was, like, hundreds of miles,
And, um, I had a bed, and all these beds lined up in one big room, and it was next to a cop, and his buddies, and Indonesians were really friendly,
And really happy, and outgoing, and really welcoming, and, uh, so I was, like, hanging out with them, like, they like to kind of, I guess,
Because I was a tourist, they, wherever you go, anywhere, anywhere you go, pretty much, you're really popular, just being American, or a tourist,
Everybody wants to hang out with you, and, so I was hanging, I hung out with them, I think it was, like, a whole day's trip,
Got to Jakarta, and then, uh, rode my bike, I was getting stoned, I think, on the boat, I think I would, like, go out,
I don't know if I did on that trip, but I think I, I, I, like, rode off on the side of the road, and got high, and then I,
I rode my bike from where the boat was, to the center of the, of Jakarta, there's this big tower, and just to the north of it was a hostel,
I think I found it from, like, the Lonely Planet book, or something, but it was really fun riding there,
Because it was in the middle of some, like, rush hour, with all these little tuk-tuks, just jam-packed together, not going anywhere,
And I was on my bike, just zooming, zigzagging through everybody, and I was kind of, like, in this zone, where I was,
Sometimes you get, when you bike to them for a really long time, where you're just, you and the bike are one,
And I was just zooming in and out, it was really fun, passing everybody, it was like there was no traffic for me,
It's like time stops for everybody else, but for you, it really keeps going, and so I went to this hotel room,
And, um, and, uh, I got this room in a, uh, in a, uh, hotel, in this, like, you know, bunk in some hotel room,
And, uh, went to sleep, and, and, uh, I got up, I remember I woke up on my bunk bed,
I was dreaming, and I wake up, and I'm in this, like, room, and for some reason, I was completely naked,
And my sheets were completely off of me, and there was this guy, like, over there, like, going through his stuff,
I was like, oh my god, Jesus, and, uh, anyways, I rode my bike around Jakarta, I did, I did the tour of the,
Of the tower, and they have some really weird, really weird, like, uh, spatial customs there,
Like, I know, in, in, like, Italy, it's like that, I was in Rome, waiting to get on, as an American,
And we actually have this thing called space, your personal space, and the subway comes up,
And then I was waiting right where the subway door opened, but these two other Italians went in before me,
And then I went in, because I guess there's not nearly as much personal space in, in Europe,
But, but with Indonesia, it's even worse, but I guess I'll continue that in the next video.
